summary:
The Cochise culture was a Desert Archaic culture of the American Southwest, primarily in present-day Arizona and New Mexico as well as adjacent areas of northern Mexico, active from approximately 7000 to 500 BCE. During the period 4000–3000 BCE, the Cochise people were hunter-gatherers who exploited a wide range of wild plant foods — particularly grass seeds ground on large stone milling slabs called manos and metates — alongside hunting deer, rabbits, and other game.

The Cochise culture is significant as a precursor to the later agricultural Mogollon tradition, and the introduction of maize cultivation into the Southwest, which likely arrived from Mesoamerica, began to supplement the Cochise subsistence base toward the late second millennium BCE. Cochise-period archaeological sites are characterized by temporary or semi-permanent camps, seed-grinding implements, and projectile points that evolved through several recognized types over millennia. The culture illustrates the adaptive strategies of desert-dwelling peoples in western North America before the adoption of agriculture.

read_more_links.title: Prehistory of the American Southwest - Smithsonian
